{{Chess Opening Theory/Position|= |Sveshnikov Sicilian| |rd| |bd|qd|kd|bd| |rd|= |pd|pd| |pd| |pd|pd|pd|= | | |nd| | |nd| | |= | |nl| | |pd| | | |= | | | | |pl| | | |= | | |nl| | | | | |= |pl|pl|pl| | |pl|pl|pl|= |rl| |bl|ql|kl|bl| |rl|= || }} = Sveshnikov Sicilian = Moves:1.e4 c5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.d4 cxd4 4.Nxd4 Nf6 5.Nc3 e5 6.Ndb5 The White knight's threat of check on d6 is a bit awkward: ...Ke7 would be unpleasant so Black would have to surrender his bishop with ...Bxd6, only to have a queen arrive on d6 and single-handedly cramp the whole Black position. She would need to go, too: ...Qe7 Qxe7 Kxe7/Nxe7, and suddenly Black's dodgy d-pawn and slow development are significant factors in the much simplified position. Far better to keep the knight out of d6 for now and then vanquish it to a3 in a move's time with [[/6...d6|6...d6]]. ==Theory table== '''1.e4 c5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.d4 cxd4 4.Nxd4 Nf6 5.Nc3 e5 6.Ndb5'''
6
